ankai: Ankai partnerships have extreme power imbalance — the an party's core competencies are continuously hollowed out
The kai party: 'nothing else is bearable' — long-term partnerships require strict IP protection and exit clauses
Once the an party transfers core technology or customer relationships, all negotiating leverage is lost
Bad year plus ankai means extremely high partnership rupture risk with irreversible losses
yusui: The sui-party company gradually loses autonomy in yusui partnerships; dependency deepens over time
The sui party discovers 'can't leave when you want to' — exit costs rise with each passing year
The yu-party company's input-output ratio deteriorates annually; resentment accumulates
Bad year plus sui party means the other can unilaterally change terms — you have no leverage
kisei: The alignment period may last longer than expected; initial process conflicts and culture clashes are normal
The ki-party company's pressure tolerance has a ceiling — beyond it, healthy complementarity becomes toxic drain
mei: Same-segment partnerships are fundamentally competitive; information leakage damage exceeds partnership benefits
In the mei position, don't proactively initiate partnerships — every insight you share may be used against you
When markets shrink, both companies fight over the same pie — partnership can instantly turn adversarial
gyotai: The gyo-party company tends to over-invest resources in this partnership, crowding out other opportunities
If industry directions diverge, the natural alignment vanishes instantly
Not seeing short-term partnership returns is normal — this requires a long-term industry positioning mindset
